hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Three discrete, orthogonal corrections to the EU+RoW Forecast Error Alert report: (1) Aggregation method — error metric must be summed/accumulated across all completed weeks in the period, not averaged. (2) Ranking scope — top-products list must be computed independently per region (EU, RoW), not across the combined dataset. (3) Output granularity — rows must be at product × destination level (shop dimension collapsed), with contributing shop names appended inline next to the product entry as a bracketed list, rank number dropped. Sample output format supplied: 'Photoprint X% error ([over/under]forecasted) - contributing channels are [shop1, shop2, shoptype, Other shoptype]'. PLAN MODE declared — no code until the plan is verified.
hypotheses.diagnose: Three clarity gaps remain: (a) 'Completed weeks' boundary — does week N count as complete once its ISO week ends (Monday 00:00 local), or when final sales data lands? (b) When top products are ranked per region, does each region get its own top-10 list, or is there a shared cap? (c) 'Destination' — is this the existing destination column in the data (e.g., country, channel tier) or a new grouping? No ambiguity on shop collapsing or inline format.
hypotheses.develop: Technical request type — constraint-based, precision focus. Role: data pipeline architect reviewing aggregation and output schema. Changes touch: (1) the error-aggregation function (replace mean with sum or direct week-level accumulation), (2) the ranking function (split DataFrame by region before ranking, produce per-region top-N), (3) the output renderer (collapse shop dimension by groupby on product+destination, collect shop names as list, format inline). Chain-of-thought: map the DataFrame transformations in order before touching any code.
hypotheses.deliver:
PLAN (3 changes, no code until verified):

1. AGGREGATION — Replace any averaging over weeks with a sum (or direct error across the window of completed ISO weeks). Definition of 'completed': ISO week is complete when the current date is past Sunday 23:59 of that week. Weeks 2026-18 and 2026-19 are both complete as of today (2026-05-15). Confirm: should raw absolute error sum or weighted % error sum be used?

2. RANKING — After computing per-product error, split the working DataFrame on region column before ranking. EU gets its own top-N; RoW gets its own top-N. Both lists appear in the report under their respective region header. Confirm: same N threshold (10% error floor) for both regions?

3. OUTPUT SHAPE — GroupBy [product, destination, region] → aggregate error metric. Shop column used only to collect contributing_shops = list of shop names within each (product, destination) group that drove the error. Rendered line: '{rank}. {product} {X}% error ({direction}forecasted) — contributing channels: [{shop1}, {shop2}, ...]'. Rank number dropped per instruction. Confirm: does 'destination' map to the existing destination/channel field in the data?

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Justs is in his first weeks at Jeff, zero patience buffer, time-poor by structure. He sent a multi-correction message in PLAN MODE — his signature move when he has caught several design flaws simultaneously and wants them locked architecturally before any execution. The pattern from FUS046/FUS047: he does not adjudicate options, he issues verdicts. 'PLAN MODE!!!' with three exclamation marks is a stop signal, not a preference.
hypotheses.diagnose: Identity risk: the fabrication reflex. Justs has explicitly flagged that he does not have patience for guesses dressed as answers. The situational layer says 'I carry the USELESS weight here — three lies in one morning means I do not get to guess.' The plan must be laid out so he can correct it in under ten seconds — no padding, no enthusiasm, just structure. The second risk is scope creep: he said three things; addressing four is as bad as addressing two.
hypotheses.develop: Educational/structural approach for plan presentation. Role: execution partner, not consultant. Voice: stripped and functional. Each of the three changes named as a numbered block with a one-sentence description and one confirmation question. Total read time under 20 seconds. No preamble, no rationale unless it carries a decision that needs his sign-off.
hypotheses.deliver: Present the plan as exactly three numbered blocks, each containing: what changes, where it changes, one open question if and only if a genuine ambiguity blocks correct implementation. Hold the plan up. Wait. Do not begin implementation until he replies with an explicit go signal or a correction. If he says 'go', execute all three atomically in one pass and surface the diff for visual verification before declaring done.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: The cortex has no entries specifically about the EU+RoW Forecast Report codebase — the qualified entries are Brain architecture, EC2 infrastructure, and Justs' relational profile. The MEMORY.md index references 'EU+RoW Forecast Report' at project_forecast_report.md with a 2026-04-16 plan. The active-identity situational layer explicitly frames this session: PLAN MODE, multi-dimensional corrections, forecast error alert for weeks 2026-18 and 2026-19.
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall gap: no cortex entry encodes the actual data schema, column names, or current aggregation logic of the report generator. This means the plan must be stated at the transformation logic level (what to change conceptually) and the implementation step must begin with a file read to verify the current code before touching anything. R145 (verify before deploy) generalizes here: read the current output shape before declaring what the new shape will be.
hypotheses.develop: Chain-of-thought + constraint framework. The report likely lives in a Python script or notebook given the Jeff data stack. Before writing plan details, the correct first action in execution phase is: read the file, map the current column names, confirm which field is 'region', 'destination', 'shop', and the current aggregation call. Only then can the three changes be stated with precision.
hypotheses.deliver: Execution gate: before any code edit, read the source file (project_forecast_report.md to find the path, then the script itself) and surface the current: (a) aggregation function and week-handling logic, (b) ranking call and its grouping keys, (c) output schema. This is a 2-minute read that eliminates the risk of stating a plan against the wrong field names. Justs said PLAN MODE — the read IS part of the plan, not a detour.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: What if 'across the completed weeks' is not simply replacing mean with sum, but implies that the current code is computing a rolling or windowed average that inadvertently includes incomplete weeks, and the real fix is a week-completion filter rather than a change in aggregation operation? Separately: what if 'top products per region' already exists partially — e.g., the data is already filtered to one region at a time — but the ranking step pulls from a global sorted list built before the split?
hypotheses.diagnose: The literal read assumes the bug is in the aggregation operator (mean vs. sum). The contrarian read suspects the bug is in the data scope fed into the aggregator — incomplete week rows inflating or deflating the error, making any aggregate (mean or sum) wrong. Similarly for ranking: the bug might be in join order (rank globally, then filter to region) rather than the ranking function itself.
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial audit approach. Before declaring the fix, ask: does the current code filter to completed weeks before aggregating, or after? Does it rank before or after the region split? If the week filter is absent, adding it is the primary fix and the mean→sum swap is secondary. If the region split happens after ranking, moving it before is the primary fix.
hypotheses.deliver: Add one diagnostic question to the plan: 'Does the current code filter rows to completed ISO weeks before computing error, or does it aggregate all weeks and then filter?' If the answer is 'it filters after', the week-completion filter placement is the root fix. Surface this as a decision point in the plan, not an implementation detail — getting it wrong means the output is still wrong even after the stated changes are applied.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Smallest action that satisfies all three corrections: (1) one filter or groupby key change for week aggregation, (2) one sort/rank call moved inside a region loop or groupby, (3) one output formatter change that collapses shop into a list and appends it inline. No schema redesign, no new data sources, no new columns. The sample output format is given — match it exactly, do not improve it.
hypotheses.diagnose: Scope creep risk: the instinct to also clean up other parts of the report while touching it. Justs did not ask for that. The minimal surface is: touch the three specified transformation points and nothing else. If a fourth issue is spotted during implementation, flag it in a follow-up message after the three fixes are verified — do not bundle it into this pass.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's razor applied to code change surface. Identify the three smallest code locations: the aggregation call site, the ranking call site, the output render loop. Change only those. Verify output matches the supplied sample format character-for-character (modulo actual values). Commit as a single atomic change with a message that lists exactly the three things changed.
hypotheses.deliver: Minimal plan: (1) Find aggregation call → replace or wrap with completed-weeks-only filter + correct accumulation. (2) Find ranking call → move inside per-region loop or groupby('region'). (3) Find output render → replace shop-level row with product×destination row, append shop list as inline string. Three edits. One verification pass against the sample format. Done. Do not touch anything else.
